part_title: Section Sixteen: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies
part_short_title: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies
part_subtitle: Biological Belts, Insectary Plants and Conservation Biological Control

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction
Sustainable management of Tuta absoluta depends on the capacity of the agricultural system to support and conserve natural enemies, not merely on releasing them or detecting them within the field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sustainable management of Tuta absoluta depends on the capacity of the agricultural system to support and conserve [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]], not merely on releasing them or detecting them within the field.

items.text: Highly uniform crop fields may provide the pest with an abundant host plant, but they do not necessarily provide parasitoids and predators with their requirements for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Highly uniform crop fields may provide the pest with an abundant [[term:host-plant]]host plant[[/term]], but they do not necessarily provide parasitoids and predators with their requirements for:

items: Plants that help them survive between pest outbreaks.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Plants that help them survive between pest outbreaks.

items.text: This is the basis of the biological belt concept: a planned vegetation structure established along field margins or within the field to improve the functional environment of natural enemies and increase their ability to reach and establish within the tomato crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the basis of the biological belt concept: a planned vegetation structure established along field margins or within the field to improve the functional environment of natural enemies and increase their ability to reach and establish within the tomato crop.

items.text: A biological belt does not function as a direct insecticide against Tuta absoluta, nor does it alone guarantee reduced infestation. Rather, it is one component of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt does not function as a direct insecticide against Tuta absoluta, nor does it alone guarantee reduced infestation. Rather, it is one component of:

items.text: Conservation Biological Control
Its principle is to protect naturally occurring natural enemies and provide the resources that allow them to survive, reproduce, and perform their function within the agroecosystem.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its principle is to protect naturally occurring natural enemies and provide the resources that allow them to survive, reproduce, and perform their function within the agroecosystem.

items.text: The biological belt must be scientifically designed because unsuitable plant selection may:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The biological belt must be scientifically designed because unsuitable plant selection may:

items: Attract natural enemies to the belt without encouraging movement into the crop.
Increase production costs without reducing fruit infestation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Attract natural enemies to the belt without encouraging movement into the crop.
[[prepared]]Increase production costs without reducing fruit infestation.

items.text: The belt should therefore not be evaluated by the abundance of flowers or insects observed on it, but by its capacity to improve biological-control indicators within the tomato crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt should therefore not be evaluated by the abundance of flowers or insects observed on it, but by its capacity to improve biological-control indicators within the tomato crop.

items.text: Definition of a Biological Belt

A biological belt is a planned vegetation area consisting of one plant species or a mixture of species, positioned around the field or in strips within it to support beneficial organisms and improve ecological functions associated with biological control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt is a planned vegetation area consisting of one plant species or a mixture of species, positioned around the field or in strips within it to support beneficial organisms and improve ecological functions associated with biological control.

items.text: It may consist of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may consist of:

items: Flowering plants around the field perimeter.
A mixture of annual plants with staggered flowering.
Plants providing nectar, pollen, or alternative prey.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A mixture of annual plants with staggered flowering.
[[prepared]]Plants providing nectar, pollen, or alternative prey.

items.text: A biological belt differs from leaving random weeds because the belt:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt differs from leaving random weeds because the belt:

items: Has a defined location and area.
Is planted at a specified time.
Is monitored for pests and diseases.
Is evaluated using defined performance indicators.

items.text: Random plants may support some natural enemies, but they may also serve as hosts for the pest or diseases or obstruct field operations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Random plants may support some natural enemies, but they may also serve as hosts for the pest or diseases or obstruct field operations.

items.text: Distinction Among Terms Associated with Biological Belts
Biological Belt
Biological Belt
A continuous or semi-continuous vegetation area around a field or one of its sectors, intended to support beneficial biodiversity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A continuous or semi-continuous vegetation area around a field or one of its sectors, intended to support beneficial biodiversity.

items.text: Flower Strip
Flower Strip
A strip planted primarily with flowering plants to provide nectar and pollen.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A strip planted primarily with flowering plants to provide nectar and pollen.

items.text: Insectary Plants
Insectary Plants
Plants selected for their capacity to provide food, shelter, or suitable conditions for parasitoids and predators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plants selected for their capacity to provide food, shelter, or suitable conditions for parasitoids and predators.

items.text: Banker Plants
Banker Plants
Plants carrying an alternative prey or host that does not damage the crop, used to maintain and multiply a natural enemy within the production system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plants carrying an alternative prey or host that does not damage the crop, used to maintain and multiply a natural enemy within the production system.

items.text: Trap Crop
Trap Crop
A plant more attractive to the pest than the main crop, planted to draw the pest away and then managed before it becomes a source of spread.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A plant more attractive to the pest than the main crop, planted to draw the pest away and then managed before it becomes a source of spread.

items.text: Companion Plant
Companion Plant
A plant grown with the crop for multiple purposes, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A plant grown with the crop for multiple purposes, such as:

items.text: Vegetative Barrier
Vegetative Barrier
A vegetation strip used to reduce wind, spray drift, or movement of some insects, without necessarily having the primary purpose of providing food resources to beneficial organisms.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A vegetation strip used to reduce wind, spray drift, or movement of some insects, without necessarily having the primary purpose of providing food resources to beneficial organisms.

items.text: These terms should not be treated as synonyms because each system has a different mechanism, risk profile, and management requirement.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These terms should not be treated as synonyms because each system has a different mechanism, risk profile, and management requirement.

items.text: Scientific Basis of Biological Belts
A biological belt functions by modifying the agricultural environment in favour of natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt functions by modifying the agricultural environment in favour of natural enemies.

items.text: Its principal mechanisms include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its principal mechanisms include:

items: 3. Providing alternative prey when Tuta absoluta density is low.
4. Providing shelter from heat, desiccation, and wind.
5. Increasing opportunities for survival and reproduction.
6. Providing vegetative connectivity that facilitates movement between habitats and the crop.
7. Accelerating recolonization of the field after disturbance.
9. Extending the period during which natural enemies remain present.
10. Improving system resilience when pest density increases.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]3. Providing alternative prey when Tuta absoluta density is low.
[[prepared]]4. Providing shelter from heat, desiccation, and wind.
[[prepared]]5. Increasing opportunities for survival and reproduction.
[[prepared]]6. Providing vegetative connectivity that facilitates movement between habitats and the crop.
[[prepared]]7. Accelerating recolonization of the field after disturbance.
[[prepared]]9. Extending the period during which natural enemies remain present.
[[prepared]]10. Improving system resilience when pest density increases.

items.text: Not all plants operate through the same mechanisms. A plant may be suitable for feeding a minute parasitoid but unsuitable for a predator requiring pollen or alternative prey.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not all plants operate through the same mechanisms. A plant may be suitable for feeding a minute parasitoid but unsuitable for a predator requiring pollen or alternative prey.

items.text: Nectar as an Energy Source
Many adult parasitoids require carbohydrate sources for flight, searching, and survival.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Many adult parasitoids require carbohydrate sources for flight, searching, and survival.

items.text: These may be obtained from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These may be obtained from:

items.text: Availability of sugar resources influences:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Availability of sugar resources influences:

items: Number of hosts it can locate.
Survival during periods of low pest density.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Survival during periods of low pest density.

items.text: The presence of nectar does not necessarily mean it can be used. The natural enemy must be able to reach it according to mouthpart length and flower structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The presence of nectar does not necessarily mean it can be used. The natural enemy must be able to reach it according to mouthpart length and flower structure.

items.text: Pollen and Alternative Prey
Some omnivorous predators benefit from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some omnivorous predators benefit from:

items: Small insects occurring on the plant.

items.text: This can help predators persist when Tuta absoluta density is low.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This can help predators persist when Tuta absoluta density is low.

items.text: However, supporting alternative prey requires monitoring because some may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, supporting alternative prey requires monitoring because some may be:

items: Sources of whitefly or thrips outbreaks.

items.text: The objective is not to multiply an alternative pest, but to maintain a limited population of harmless resources or to use suitable food plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The objective is not to multiply an alternative pest, but to maintain a limited population of harmless resources or to use suitable food plants.

items.text: Shelter and Microclimate
The belt may provide:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt may provide:

items.text: These functions are especially important in open fields exposed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These functions are especially important in open fields exposed to:

items: Disturbance from spraying and mechanized operations.

items.text: An excessively dense belt may, however, increase humidity or obstruct ventilation and movement. A balance is therefore required between providing shelter and avoiding excessive density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An excessively dense belt may, however, increase humidity or obstruct ventilation and movement. A balance is therefore required between providing shelter and avoiding excessive density.

The belt may be designed to support groups including:

Such as:

Such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such as:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some of these species require carbohydrates to maintain activity, while obtaining protein through host feeding.

Such as:

Such as:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single plant supports all of these groups equally.

items.text: Biological Belts Do Not Kill Tuta absoluta Directly
A distinction must be made between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction must be made between:

items: Increasing natural enemies within the belt.
Increasing control within the tomato crop.

items.text: A belt may attract many beneficial insects without causing sufficient movement into the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A belt may attract many beneficial insects without causing sufficient movement into the crop.

items.text: Parasitoid abundance may increase at the field edge without a clear decline in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitoid abundance may increase at the field edge without a clear decline in:

items.text: The belt should therefore not be declared successful merely because insect diversity increases. Its effect must be demonstrated within the tomato crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt should therefore not be declared successful merely because insect diversity increases. Its effect must be demonstrated within the tomato crop.

items.text: Field studies have shown that flower strips increased parasitoid abundance and some beneficial insects, while pest incidence within the crop did not decline significantly in all cases.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field studies have shown that flower strips increased parasitoid abundance and some beneficial insects, while pest incidence within the crop did not decline significantly in all cases.

items.text: This confirms that the belt is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This confirms that the belt is:

items: Not a substitute for management of source fields.
Not a substitute for early intervention.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Not a substitute for management of source fields.

items.text: Criteria for Selecting Belt Plants
Plant selection should be based on an integrated set of criteria.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant selection should be based on an integrated set of criteria.

items.text: Biological Criteria

items: Provision of food for the target natural enemy.
No substantial increase in survival or fecundity of Tuta absoluta.
Unsuitability as a host for the pest.
Support for more than one beneficial group where possible.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Provision of food for the target natural enemy.
[[prepared]]No substantial increase in survival or fecundity of Tuta absoluta.
[[prepared]]Unsuitability as a host for the pest.
[[prepared]]Support for more than one beneficial group where possible.

items.text: Agronomic Criteria

items: Tolerance of soil and water conditions.
Capacity to flower during the tomato season.
Low risk of becoming a difficult weed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Capacity to flower during the tomato season.
[[prepared]]Low risk of becoming a difficult weed.

items.text: Operational Criteria

items: Ability to provide a separate irrigation line.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability to provide a separate irrigation line.

items.text: Economic Criteria
Flower Structure and Nectar Accessibility
Many insect parasitoids have short mouthparts. Shallow flowers or exposed inflorescences are therefore more usable than deep flowers.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Many insect parasitoids have short mouthparts. Shallow flowers or exposed inflorescences are therefore more usable than deep flowers.

items.text: Some plants in the family Apiaceae have open inflorescences bearing many small flowers, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some plants in the family Apiaceae have open inflorescences bearing many small flowers, including:

items.text: Plants with small clustered inflorescences may also provide nectar suitable for several parasitoids.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plants with small clustered inflorescences may also provide nectar suitable for several parasitoids.

items.text: Total nectar quantity alone is insufficient. A plant may produce abundant nectar in a location inaccessible to minute insects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Total nectar quantity alone is insufficient. A plant may produce abundant nectar in a location inaccessible to minute insects.

items.text: Selectivity in Favour of the Natural Enemy
The plant should benefit the natural enemy more than the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant should benefit the natural enemy more than the pest.

items.text: Some adult moths may also benefit from sugars and nectar, which may:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some adult moths may also benefit from sugars and nectar, which may:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant should therefore be tested for effects on:

items.text: Tests on several flowering plants showed that some flowers benefited larval parasitoids of Tuta more than they benefited adult pests, but results differed among plant species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tests on several flowering plants showed that some flowers benefited larval parasitoids of Tuta more than they benefited adult pests, but results differed among plant species.

items.text: Selectivity cannot be assumed without testing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Selectivity cannot be assumed without testing.

items.text: Temporal Succession of Flowering
The belt fails when all plants flower briefly and finish before the main risk period.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt fails when all plants flower briefly and finish before the main risk period.

items.text: The design should aim for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The design should aim for:

items: Continued resources during vegetative growth and fruiting.
Inclusion of fast and longer-lived species.
Cutting parts of the belt without removing it all.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Continued resources during vegetative growth and fruiting.
[[prepared]]Cutting parts of the belt without removing it all.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Continuous flowering is not necessarily required after the crop ends if the belt would become a pest source or obstruct field termination.

items.text: Exclusion of Hazardous Host Plants
Plants capable of supporting Tuta absoluta or major tomato pests should be avoided unless a clear management plan exists.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plants capable of supporting Tuta absoluta or major tomato pests should be avoided unless a clear management plan exists.

items.text: Particularly avoid solanaceous plants that may act as hosts, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Particularly avoid solanaceous plants that may act as hosts, such as:

items.text: The plant should also be assessed for potential hosting of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant should also be assessed for potential hosting of:

items.text: A solanaceous trap crop should not be used without a strict protocol for eliminating the pest within it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A solanaceous trap crop should not be used without a strict protocol for eliminating the pest within it.

items.text: Dill
Anethum graveolens
Dill is a candidate biological-belt plant because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dill is a candidate biological-belt plant because of:

items: Potential access of parasitoids and minute insects to nectar.
Relative suitability for winter and spring cultivation in Egypt.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Potential access of parasitoids and minute insects to nectar.
[[prepared]]Relative suitability for winter and spring cultivation in Egypt.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its success is influenced by:

items.text: Coriander
Coriandrum sativum
Coriander is a strong candidate because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Coriander is a strong candidate because of:

items: Potential as an additional marketable crop.
Inclusion in floral mixtures that attracted parasitoids and beneficial insects in tomato fields.

items.text: Some studies also associated coriander intercropping with reduced infestation or increased predators in particular systems.

items.text: These findings do not demonstrate that coriander suppresses Tuta absoluta under all conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These findings do not demonstrate that coriander suppresses Tuta absoluta under all conditions.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be evaluated:

items: Hosting of aphids or other insects.
Movement of beneficial organisms into the crop.

Foeniculum vulgare
Fennel has open umbel inflorescences and can provide food resources over a relatively extended period.

Tagetes spp.

Calendula officinalis
Calendula is sometimes used to support or conserve certain predators around tomato systems.

items.text: Calculating Seed Quantity
Seed quantity is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Seed quantity is calculated as:

items.text: Required seed quantity = Area allocated to the species × Recommended seed rate per unit area
Where correction for purity and germination is required:

items.text: Required commercial seed = Required pure live seed ÷ (Decimal purity × Decimal germination)

items.text: Biological-Belt Design Forms
Four principal designs may be used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Four principal designs may be used.

items.text: Complete External Belt
This extends around all field sides.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This extends around all field sides.

items.text: Advantages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advantages:

items: Reception of natural enemies from the surrounding environment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reception of natural enemies from the surrounding environment.

items.text: Limitations:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limitations:

items: May obstruct movement around the field.

items.text: Belt on Selected Sides
This is placed along:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is placed along:

items: The side adjacent to natural habitats.
The side least conflicting with machinery.
Areas that can be protected from spray.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Areas that can be protected from spray.

items.text: It is less costly but provides less connectivity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is less costly but provides less connectivity.

items.text: Internal Strips
These are established between field sectors.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are established between field sectors.

items.text: Advantages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advantages:

items: Reduce the distance between resources and the crop.
Improve movement of beneficial organisms into the field interior.

[[prepared]]Improve movement of beneficial organisms into the field interior.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limitations:

items.text: Vegetation Islands
These are small patches distributed within the field or at road intersections.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are small patches distributed within the field or at road intersections.

items.text: Advantages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advantages:

items: No need for a long strip.
Can be positioned near risk zones.

items.text: However, they may provide less connectivity than continuous strips.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, they may provide less connectivity than continuous strips.

items.text: Position of the Belt Relative to the Infestation Source
A belt should not automatically be placed beside a heavily infested field with the intention of stopping moths, because it is not a guaranteed barrier.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A belt should not automatically be placed beside a heavily infested field with the intention of stopping moths, because it is not a guaranteed barrier.

Allow direct pest movement into tomato.
Increase difficulty of protecting the belt from insecticides.
Turn it into a mixed zone of pests and beneficial organisms.

[[prepared]]Increase difficulty of protecting the belt from insecticides.
[[prepared]]Turn it into a mixed zone of pests and beneficial organisms.

To avoid simultaneous termination of flowering, the dill and coriander areas may be divided into two or three sowing batches.

items.text: The Belt Is Not a Trap Crop for Tuta
Dill, coriander, or basil should not be assumed to attract Tuta absoluta away from tomato as trap crops.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dill, coriander, or basil should not be assumed to attract Tuta absoluta away from tomato as trap crops.

items.text: The belt should therefore not be described as “pulling Tuta away” without evidence.

A banker plant differs from a flower strip because it carries an alternative host or prey that permits natural-enemy multiplication.

items.text: Basil and the Push–Pull Strategy
Basil may represent a potential component of the “push” element if it is shown to reduce oviposition or female attraction.

items.text: Belt-Management Decision Matrix

rows: Good flowering and increased beneficial organisms within tomato
Continue management
Good flowering but beneficial organisms remain confined to the belt
Modify the design or add internal connectivity
Increased aphids without sufficient natural enemies
Remove or apply selective localized treatment
Appearance of whiteflies or virus vectors
Immediate assessment and source reduction
Flowering ends too early
Establish a new batch or use staggered cutting
Clear competition with tomato
Reduce belt width or separate irrigation
Obstruction of spraying or harvesting
Modify location or design
Repeated pesticide exposure of the belt
Establish a protection zone or change the application route
No reduction in Tuta despite increased beneficial organisms
Continue IPM and do not rely on the belt alone
High cost without measurable benefit
Redesign or discontinue the uneconomic component

[[prepared]]Good flowering but beneficial organisms remain confined to the belt
[[prepared]]Modify the design or add internal connectivity
[[prepared]]Establish a new batch or use staggered cutting
[[prepared]]Establish a protection zone or change the application route
[[prepared]]No reduction in Tuta despite increased beneficial organisms
[[prepared]]Continue IPM and do not rely on the belt alone

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Treating the Belt as a Direct Insecticide
Its role is to support the biological system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its role is to support the biological system.

items.text: Error Two: Planting Any Flowering Species
Host status, associated pests, and nectar accessibility must be evaluated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Host status, associated pests, and nectar accessibility must be evaluated.

items.text: Error Three: Treating the 50:30:20 Ratio as a Fixed Scientific Fact
It is an experimental model requiring validation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is an experimental model requiring validation.

items.text: Error Four: Calculating Proportions by Seed Weight
They should be calculated by area or strip length.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should be calculated by area or strip length.

items.text: Error Five: Establishing the Belt After Infestation Has Increased
The belt requires early establishment and flowering.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt requires early establishment and flowering.

items.text: Error Six: Allowing All Plants to Finish Flowering at the Same Time
Staggered planting should be used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Staggered planting should be used.

items.text: Error Seven: Spraying the Belt with Broad-Spectrum Pesticides
This destroys its principal function.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This destroys its principal function.

items.text: Error Eight: Leaving the Belt Unmonitored
It may become a pest source.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may become a pest source.

items.text: Error Nine: Using Solanaceous Plants That Host Tuta
They may increase the pest instead of suppressing it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may increase the pest instead of suppressing it.

items.text: Error Ten: Measuring Insects on Flowers Only
Movement into tomato should also be measured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Movement into tomato should also be measured.

items.text: Error Eleven: Ignoring Lost Crop Area
This should be included in economic evaluation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This should be included in economic evaluation.

items.text: Error Twelve: Overfertilizing the Belt
This delays flowering and increases sucking pests.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This delays flowering and increases sucking pests.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Cutting the Entire Belt at Once
This abruptly removes resources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This abruptly removes resources.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Describing Basil as a Guaranteed Repellent
Field evidence requires local validation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field evidence requires local validation.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Discontinuing Other IPM Methods
The belt is a supporting component, not a stand-alone programme.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt is a supporting component, not a stand-alone programme.

items.text: Research and Practical Priorities in Egypt

items: 1. Test dill, coriander, and basil individually and in mixtures.
2. Measure the accessibility of their flowers to Tuta parasitoids.
3. Determine flowering schedules across different production cycles.
4. Study hosting of whiteflies, thrips, and aphids.
5. Measure movement of natural enemies from the belt into tomato.
6. Evaluate Trichogramma near and far from the belt.
7. Evaluate Necremnus tutae and local parasitoids.
9. Compare external belts with internal strips.
10. Determine the minimum area providing economic benefit.
13. Test buckwheat, sweet alyssum, and local plants.
16. Calculate costs for one and ten feddans.
17. Evaluate the potential to market belt plants.
18. Measure effects over more than one season.
20. Develop locally data-based recommendations rather than assumed ratios.

items.text: Scientific Summary of Section Sixteen
A biological belt is a planned vegetation structure intended to support and conserve natural enemies within the agroecosystem.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt is a planned vegetation structure intended to support and conserve natural enemies within the agroecosystem.

items.text: It operates through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It operates through:

items: Increased survival of parasitoids and predators.

items.text: A biological belt is not a direct method for killing Tuta absoluta and does not alone guarantee reduced infestation or fruit protection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A biological belt is not a direct method for killing Tuta absoluta and does not alone guarantee reduced infestation or fruit protection.

items.text: The natural enemy should benefit from the plant more than the pest does, nectar should be accessible, and flowering should coincide with periods of beneficial-organism activity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The natural enemy should benefit from the plant more than the pest does, nectar should be accessible, and flowering should coincide with periods of beneficial-organism activity.

items.text: Studies have shown that buckwheat and sweet alyssum were among the most beneficial plants for some larval parasitoids of Tuta, particularly Necremnus tutae and Bracon nigricans, although parasitoid responses differed among species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have shown that buckwheat and sweet alyssum were among the most beneficial plants for some larval parasitoids of Tuta, particularly Necremnus tutae and Bracon nigricans, although parasitoid responses differed among species.

items.text: Field studies also showed that flowering mixtures may increase parasitoid and beneficial-insect abundance along processing-tomato field margins, but increased beneficial abundance does not always result in a direct decline in pest incidence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field studies also showed that flowering mixtures may increase parasitoid and beneficial-insect abundance along processing-tomato field margins, but increased beneficial abundance does not always result in a direct decline in pest incidence.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A mixture of:

items.text: Is proposed for testing as an experimental Egyptian model, with proportions calculated by area or strip length rather than seed weight.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Is proposed for testing as an experimental Egyptian model, with proportions calculated by area or strip length rather than seed weight.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Belt design should include:

items.text: The belt should not be considered successful merely because flowers or beneficial insects are present. Success requires demonstrated improvement in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The belt should not be considered successful merely because flowers or beneficial insects are present. Success requires demonstrated improvement in:

items.text: The biological belt is not field decoration. It is a functional component requiring scientific design, management, and measurement.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The biological belt is not field decoration. It is a functional component requiring scientific design, management, and measurement.
